source_metadata.title_raw: Robert N. Black - A-19-103 - June 12, 2019 - Eureka

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robert N. Black 
City Attorney of Eureka 
531 K Street 
Eureka, CA 95501-1165 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-103 
Dear Mr. Black: 
June 12, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Eureka City Councilmember 
Natalie Arroyo regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 Please note that we do not advise 
on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case 
or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Humboldt County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(J).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Under Section 1090, if Councilmember Arroyo has no role and does nothing to influence 
the Purchasing Agent, may the Purchasing Agent enter into a "below-the-threshold" contract with 
Redwood Community Action Agency ("RCAA")? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Section 1090 prohibits Councilmember Arroyo from making or participating in the 
making of the potential agreements with RCAA; however, the Purchasing Agent, may enter into 
those agreements on behalf of the City, so long as Councilmember Arroyo has no role and does 
nothing to influence the Purchasing Agent. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, 
unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 
through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of 
the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-103 
Page No. 2 
Councilmember Natalie Arroyo was elected to the Eureka City Council in 2014 and is 
currently serving in that capacity. Councilmember Arroyo has worked at RCCA, a 50l(c)(3) non-
profit organization, as a senior planner in the natural resources services division since 2007. She is 
an hourly employee and works slightly less than fulltime. RCAA is a source of income of more than 
$500 annually to Councilmember Arroyo. She does not receive bonuses except longevity bonuses 
awarded to all similarly situated employees and there is no profit sharing arrangement. 
Councilmember Arroyo does not have management or development responsibilities within the 
nonprofit organization. Following her election to the Eureka City Council, her work at RCAA is 
exclusively on projects outside of Eureka city limits. Currently, her work includes community 
health projects on the Samoa peninsula and in Bridgeville (southoostern Humboldt County). 
RCAA currently has a budget of about $8 million annually. The number of employees 
within its many divisions and AmeriCorps programs is in excess of one hundred and twenty-five 
individuals, although it has varied based on grant-funded programs and some short-term or 
seasonal employment. RCAA has been in existence since 1983 with the goal of improving the lives 
of low-income people. 
The City of Eureka is a charter city. Under its home rule powers, the City has adopted a 
subchapter of its Municipal Code that empowers the City Manager, acting as Purchasing Agent 
of the City, or the City Manager's designees to acquire public works, goods, and services without . 
specific approval of the City Council below certain dollar thresholds.2 The basic precondition is that 
the City budget must contain the necessary appropriation for the acquisition in question. Above the 
thresholds, City Council approval is required. There.are several categories of goods and services 
that fall under the Purchasing Agent's authority, each with its own dollar limits, and they include 
the following: 
§ 39.39 GOODS AND SERVICES. 
(D) Author'ity to approve contracts. All Contracts for goods and services valued at 
one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) or more require Council approval. 
(1) The City Manager is authorized, without Council approval, to approve 
and award a contract for goods and services valued at less than one hundred 
thousand dollars ($100,000). 
(2) The City Manager is authorized to delegate, in writing, authority to 
approve a contract for goods and services in an amount of ten thousand dollars 
($10,000} or less. 
§ 39.40 PROFESSIONAL SERVICES. 
2 In addition, Section 602 (g) of the Municipal Code states that the City Manager "shall have the power and 
duty" to "[a]ct as purchasing agent for the city and all offices." 


File No. A-19-103 
Page No. 3 
(B) Negotiation procedure. For projects involving fees less than $100,000 where 
local business are well qualified to provide services, the Purchasing Agent, or 
designated Department may negotiate with a firm for services subject to prior 
approval from the Council. Otherwise, the Purchasing Agent shall utilize a 
request for qualifications or a request for proposals procedure for obtaining 
professional services. 
(C) Request for qualifications procedure. For projects involving fees of more than 
$100,000 or where it is in the city's best interest to request qualifications for 
services involving specialized area of expertise, a request for qualifications 
procedure shall be utilized. 
(D) Council approved required. All contracts for professional services more than 
$25,000 shall be approved by the Council. 
Because RCAA provides a wide-range of community services, elements of the 
organization are often called upon to provide service to the City of Eureka. Examples include 
having RCAA facilitate public participation in planning endeavors and the production of 
interpretive signs for a City recreational trail. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig 
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 3 8 
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to.one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
Notably, when members of a public board, commission or similar body have the power to 
execute contracts, each member is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of all 
contracts by his or her agency regardless of whether the member actually participates in the making 
of the contract. (Thomson v. Call, supra at pp. 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of 
Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).) 
The question here is whether the City Manager has independent authority to contract with 
RCAA, thus avoiding a Section 1090 violation because the City Council would not be participating 
in the making of the contract. An Attorney General opinion found a County Supervisor's ownership 
in a business did not cause a violation of Section 1090 where a county employee has independent 
legal authority to approve of transactions with the business. In that case, Alpine County wished to 


File No. A-19-103 
Page No. 4 
obtain towing services and service station supplies from a County Supervisor who owned the only 
service station in a certain area of the County to avoid having to obtain them elsewhere. (57 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 458, 459 (1974).) The County Board of Supervisors had the authority to hire a 
County Purchasing Agent under Section 25500. (Ibid.) Section 25501 set forth the Purchasing 
Agent's authority to make such purchases. (Id. at p. 460.) The Opinion concluded that the County 
Purchasing Agent had independent authority to contract, and thus could execute a contract with the 
County Supervisor for goods or services without violating Section 1090 because the Board of 
Supervisors would not be participating in the making of the contract. (Id. at p. 460-61; see also 21 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 90 (1953) [City Treasurer can deposit funds ip a bank in which a City Council 
member was a stockholder and director. "The significant fact in each of these opinions is the 
independent status of the party contracting on behalf of the governmental agency." (Id. at p. 92; 14 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 78 (1949); 3 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 188 (1944)].) 
Here, Section 34851 authorizes a City Manager form of government to be established by 
ordinance. When such ordinance is established, Section 34852 requires that it "define the powers 
and duties of the City Manager and may fix his compensation or the minimum amount he is to 
receive." 
Eureka is a charter city, with a City Manager form of government. As required by Section 
34852, the City's Municipal Code sets forth the powers and duties of the City Manager. As noted 
above, Municipal Code Sections 39.39 and 39.40 authorize the City Manager to enter into contract 
for goods and services under a specific amount without the approval of the City Council.3 
Accordingly, Councilmember Arroyo and the Eureka City Council will avoid violating 
Section 1090 where the City Manager enters a contract with RCAA, for goods and services valued 
at less than $100,000, without involvement or oversight by the City Council, pursuant to his or her 
authority under Section 34852 and Municipal Code Sections 39.39 and 39.40. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
Q~; Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
3 See also the Walter Advice Letter, No. A-15-050, concluding that a city manager, with independent statutory 
authority to approve contracts without city council approval, could approve a contract with a source of income to a 
council member, without violating Section 1090, as long as there was no involvement or oversight by the city council.

sections.question:
Under Section 1090, if Councilmember Arroyo has no role and does nothing to influence 
the Purchasing Agent, may the Purchasing Agent enter into a "below-the-threshold" contract with

sections.conclusion:
Yes. Section 1090 prohibits Councilmember Arroyo from making or participating in the 
making of the potential agreements with RCAA; however, the Purchasing Agent, may enter into 
those agreements on behalf of the City, so long as Councilmember Arroyo has no role and does 
nothing to influence the Purchasing Agent. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5.

1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5.
